topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered as I pulled the overflowing bin onto the porch. Amidst the usual stack of junk flyers and utility bills, something caught my eye. It was a card, thick cardstock, bearing no address, no name, just a cheerful, looping script wishing "Happy Birthday." My fingers, usually steady, trembled as I picked it up. The paper felt strangely warm under my touch. A knot tightened in my chest, a sudden, cold clench that made my breath catch in my throat. Who had this? Why was it here? I smoothed the card flat, the bright, unfamiliar handwriting mocking the quiet emptiness of my afternoon. I dropped it instantly, the sound barely a rustle, and retreated indoors, pacing the length of the living room, my gaze constantly flicking toward the front door, as if expecting the sender to materialize.

[story 2]

I was sorting through the morning’s correspondence, the mundane rhythm of sorting envelopes usually a small comfort. Then I saw it—a card, pristine and sealed, tucked amongst the junk mail. My heart gave a sudden, hard thump against my ribs. I picked it up, and the paper seemed to absorb the light. The message inside was short, intensely personal, and utterly anonymous. A wave of disorientation washed over me. My hands started shaking uncontrollably; I had to grip the edges of the card to keep it from slipping. Every nerve ending felt hyper-alert, prickling with a sharp, unsettling awareness. I stared at the blank space where a name should have been, my jaw tight, trying to process the sheer impossibility of the situation. I set the card down on the counter with exaggerated care, needing physical distance from the strange object.

[story 3]

The sound of the mailbox being emptied was loud in the quiet house. When I reached for the remaining items, my hand brushed against something unexpected—a card. It was unsigned, a vibrant splash of color against the dull grey of the paper. As I unfolded it, a strange pressure built behind my eyes. I read the inscription, and a sudden, sharp intake of air left my lungs. My entire body seemed to seize up; I couldn't move, my feet rooted to the floor. A frantic energy surged through me, making my palms sweat. I looked around the room wildly, searching for an explanation, for a clue, but there was only silence and the card, an undeniable mystery resting in my hand, leaving me feeling utterly unbalanced and profoundly unsettled.
